Ichino Torii
]] '''Yoshida Shrine'' (Yoshida Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}{{ill|lt=Mito City|Q204249|WD=Q204249}} Miyauchi Town. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}), {{ill|lt=Hitachikuni|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya}}, {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Overview === It is located on top of Mt. Asahi, a small hill in the southern part of central Mito city. Its founding tells of {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru}}'s involvement in the eastern expedition. Because it enshrines the god of war, Japan Takeru, it is a shrine that has long been highly revered by the Imperial Court during the {{ill|lt=Ezo|Q932574|WD=Q932574}} conquest process. Although the shrine building was burnt down in the war, a place related to Nihon Takeru still remains within the precincts as ``Mt. Sankaku.'' === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) *: The 12th {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} prince and father of the 14th {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}}. According to "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031}}" and "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}," he subjugated the powerful clans of Kyushu and eastern regions on the orders of Emperor Keiko. === History === === Founding === According to ``Joyo Shikinai Chinza Honki'' and ``Hitachi Twenty-eight Shrine Considerations,'' it is said that {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru}} rested his soldiers at this place (Mt. Asahi/Mt. Misumi) during his eastern expedition, and the shrine was built in honor of this {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. The date of its establishment is unknown, but according to ancient documents in the possession of Yoshida Shrine, it was enshrined in {{ill|lt=Shoan|Q1327427|WD=Q1327427}}4 ({{ill|lt=1301|Q5610|WD=Q5610}}). There is a note stating that more than 800 years have passed {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}[http://www.yoshidajinja.jp/hist.html]. The regular autumn festival held on October 19 and 20, 1985 was held as a celebration of the 1500th anniversary of the founding of the company.Iharaki Shimbun, morning edition of October 21, 1985. . The location of the shrine's resting place is still told today in a corner of the temple grounds. Concerning this tradition, there is a theory that the connection to Nihon Takeru dates back to the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, since there is no story about Nippon Takeru in Naga District in ``{{ill|lt=Hitachikuni Fudoki|Q11481687|WD=Q11481687}}'' {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. === Outline === First seen in national history is {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} In 13 years ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454}}), he was ranked as a junior fifth rank, lower rank, and eighth rank. It is written that ``Yoshida God'' in } was listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha #Meishin Name|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Meishin}} {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. After that, the divine rank was {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} first year ({{ill|lt=857|Q30374|WD=Q30374}}), and was given the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Rank, and Eighth Rank, {{jalink|Jōgan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}}5 years ({{ill|lt=863 years|Q25052|WD=Q25052}}) Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Rank 8th Class, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Genkei|1=UNKNOWN}}2 ({{ill|lt=878|Q23910|WD=Q23910}}), he was promoted to Seishi Rank, Lower Rank, 8th Class {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}.{{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) {{ill|lt=In the Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shin Name Book}}, "Yoshida Shrine It is listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}{{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. In addition, Yoshida Shrine is {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|WD=Q706499}} ({{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}) in Hitachi Province, {{ill|lt=Shizukami It is said to have been ranked as the third shrine after Shri|Q11663648|WD=Q11663648}} (Ninomiya), and was built in the year 2000, following Kashima Shrine {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system|2000|p=233}}. The reason why it was ranked 3rd in Hitachi Province and 1st in Naka District (Naka District) is said to be due to its relationship with Japan Takeru, the enshrined god of war, and the Ezo conquest, as well as its relationship with the decline of the Ujibe clan in Naga District, which was dominant in the west of Mito.{{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}In the Middle Ages, ``Yoshida County'' was separated from Naga County as the {{ill|lt=Kamigun|Q11590937|WD=Q11590937}} of Yoshida Shrine, but the territory of Yoshida Shrine was 158 towns, covering half of Yoshida County {{Sfn|Yoshida County (Taira)|1982}}. From then on, this shrine territory strengthened its character as a manor, and was also called ``Yoshida Manor'' {{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}}. In addition, we call Yakuo-in Temple {{ill|lt=Jingu-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}, and it is said that the shrine had 6-ken and 3-ken corridors, torii gates, and tamagaki, as well as many attached buildings and subsidiary shrines {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}, and that the shrine reached the rank of Shoichii {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. After that, Yakuo-in flourished as the center of {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|WD=Q668188}}, controversy increased, and the divine power declined {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, he was deeply revered by the {{ill|lt=Mito domain|Q1146904|WD=Q1146904}}, especially {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsukuni Tokugawa|1=UNKNOWN}} repaired the main shrine, worship hall, and many other shrine buildings in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}}6 ({{ill|lt=1666|Q7585|WD=Q7585}}).{{Sf n|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}, {{ill|lt=Nariaki Tokugawa|Q1323770|WD=Q1323770}} was founded in {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}}15 ({{jalink {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}} who donated ``{{ill|lt=Dainippon History|Q1156003|WD=Q1156003}}'' and 100 koku shrine territory to |1844|Q7633}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in April of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226}} in {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. In {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}}), the entire shrine building was burnt down in an air raid {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. The shrine building was rebuilt in 1948 ({{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|WD=Q5165}}), and has undergone subsequent renovations and repairs to its current state {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. === God level === Record of {{ill|lt=Divine Rank|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}} dedication in {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}}. Both names are written as ``Yoshida God''. * {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jowa}} On April 17, 13 years ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454}}), he was entrusted to the Meishin as a junior fifth rank, lower rank, and eighth rank. * {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} May 26, 1st year ({{ill|lt=857|Q30374|WD=Q30374}}), from the 8th grade of the Junior Fifth Rank to the 8th grade of the Lower Rank (Junior 4th Rank, Lower Rank, 8th Grade) * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052}}) August 2nd, from the 8th grade of the junior fourth rank to the 8th grade of the superior rank of the junior fourth rank (``{{ill|lt=Journal of the Third Generation of Japan|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}'') * August 8, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=878|Q23910|WD=Q23910}}), from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Rank, Eighth Class to Sei Fourth Rank, Lower Rank, Lower Rank, Eighth Class (``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'') === Shinto priest === Yoshida Shrine's social affairs are said to have been handled by ``Kimikoji'' in ancient times {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}. The origin and personality of the Yoshimi-kou clan are not clear, but it is said that he belonged to the Emishi tribe in the early Nara period, and there is also a theory that points to a deep relationship with the Emishi {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. After that, Mr. Yoshimiko was appointed to the Central Government during the {{ill|lt=Chojo|Q1194264|WD=Q1194264}} years ({{ill|lt=1132|Q19582|WD=Q19582}}-{{ill|lt=1135|Q19594|WD=Q19594}}). {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}{{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}} who donated a social affairs position (Ryoke position) to the family'''{{ill|lt=Otsuki clan|Q11462133|WD=Q11462133}}'''. This donation is said to have been made to protect the shrine's territory from the interference of local government officials and the invasion of local clans {{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}}. Through subsequent changes, there were five former shrine families (the Tadokoro clan, the Akutsu clan, the Sakaki clan, the Watanabe clan, and the Inoue clan) before the Meiji period. After the Shake system was abolished by the Constitution of the Empire of Japan, the Tadokoro clan was in charge until the middle of the Meiji period, and the Akutsu clan thereafter. After the enactment of the Religious Corporations Law after the war, the Akutsu clan was in charge until the early Heisei period after passing through several Shinto priests, and the Tadokoro clan is currently in charge. === Precincts === The main shrine has {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} copper plate roofing, and the worship hall and the shrine have {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}} copper plate roofing, both of which were rebuilt after the war {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. On the approach to the shrine, there is a place consecrated as the ``Asahi Sankakuyama Ruins'' where the enshrined deity Nihon Takeru rested {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. This triangular mountain is said to be the site of the main shrine and rituals {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. Kasahara Yasu Shrine (Kasahara Town)
]] [[File:Sakado-jinja (Mito) haiden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|
Sakamon Shrine (Sakamon Town)
]] "Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi" describes Kasahara Daimyojin and Sakamon Daimyojin as separate shrines. ; Kasahara Yasu Shrine (Kasahara Daimyojin) :* Enshrinement location: Ichiban, Kasahara-cho, Mito City ({{Coord|36|21|5.17|N|140|27|18.13|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Bekku: Kasaharayasu Shrine}}) :* Deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ototachibanahime|UNKNOWN|lt=younger brother Tachibanahime}}, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuyahime|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuyahime}}: A separate shrine of Yoshida Shrine and one of the seven shrines in Mito. During the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}} years ({{ill|lt=1661|Q7005|WD=Q7005}}-{{ill|lt=1673|Q7617|WD=Q7617}}), a shrine was built at the current location on the orders of the founder of the Mito domain, {{ill|lt=Yorifusa Tokugawa|Q1133680|WD=Q1133680}}, and it was said to be the guardian deity of safe childbirth. It is said that there was a tradition of borrowing a statue from the head office when giving birth, and then returning it and offering it after a safe delivery. The offspring were Chinami, Mikawa, and Kasahara. Kasahara Shrine became a religious corporation in September 1952 ({{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272}}), and is now known as ``Kasahara Yasu Shrine'' under the jurisdiction of the umbrella organization {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|WD=Q908077}}. : According to the documents owned by Yoshida Shrine, on February 24, {{ill|lt=Enkei (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkei}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1309|Q5726|WD=Q5726}}), the then Saemon-jo and Uemon-jo issued a reply regarding ``Yoshida Kasahara Shrine Transfer Shrine.'' Furthermore, on May 25, 1996 ({{ill|lt=1396|Q6405|WD=Q6405}}), Ujiyoshi Sani sent out a letter of donation stating, ``Kasahara Gosha Gotodensho Hōdonoya'' (from ``Suifu Geion Koroki''). ; Sakamon Shrine (Sakamon Daimyojin) :* Enshrinement location: Sakamon-cho, Mito City ({{Coord|36|21|22.47|N|140|29|52.64|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Bekku: Sakamon Shrine}}) :* Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuyahime|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohana Sakuyahime}} === Precincts Suesha === * Rokusha (on the right side facing the main shrine) ** Matsuo Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302}} in {{ill|lt=Kyoto|Q740246|WD=Q740246}}. ** Taga Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091}} in {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga}}. ** Sumiyoshi Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|WD=Q11381857}} in {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906}}. ** Hachiman Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Hachiman Shrine|Q11390939|WD=Q11390939}}. ** Okunishushi Daishu Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Okunishu no Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Okunishushi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kotoshiro no Mikoto|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshiro no Mikoto}}. ** Small Pox Guardian Shrine * Two shrines (back right facing the main shrine) *: A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. Both companies are mentioned in the ``Chinjucho'' and ``Jinhachisha Ryakuenshi.'' ** Kunimi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Hikokuni Mikaki Kenyotsuka no Mikoto. {{ill|lt=Ise Jingu|Q687168|WD=Q687168}}He is the enshrined deity of Geku Setsusha and {{ill|lt=Watari Kunomi Shrine|Q11486118|WD=Q11486118}}, and the ancestor of Ise Kunizo and Watarai Shinto priests. ** Hayatoshi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Ryodoirihime no Mikoto. One of the wives of Takeru of Japan and the mother of Emperor Chuai. * Two shrines (back left facing the main shrine) *: A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. Both companies are mentioned in the ``Chinjucho'' and ``Jinhachisha Ryakuenshi.'' ** Ii Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}}. He was the second prince of Japan Takeru, and his mother was Ryodo Irihime no Mikoto.**: "Shingusha" is written in "Chinjucho". The ``Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi'' describes it as ``Ii Shrine'' and ``Kehi Myojin''. ** Mito Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhiko|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayaakitsuhiko no Mikoto}}. A branch of Mito Shrine in Kasahara-cho, Mito City. * Two shrines (Kodaijingu Shrine and Toyouke Daijingu Shrine) *: "Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi" says "Ise Nisho Bekku". A branch of {{ill|lt=Ise Jingu|Q687168|WD=Q687168}}{{ill|lt=Kōtai Jingu|Q11581011|WD=Q11581011|lt=Naiku}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Geku|1=UNKNOWN}}. The shrine building is Nigensha {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}}. ** Kodai Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu}}. ** Toyouke Daijingu Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Toyouke Hime|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Hime no Mikoto}}. * Inari Shrine/Hashi Shrine *: In "Shuhachisha Ryoenshi", it is only described as {{ill|lt=Inari God|Q719665|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Myojin}}. A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. * Hoshimiya Shrine * Tenmangu - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|WD=Q382005}} *: ``Chinjucho'' says ``Tenjin Shrine,'' and ``Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi'' says ``Tenma Tenjin.'' While all the other subsidiary shrines have small shrine buildings, Tenmangu Shrine has a medium-sized, flowing-style shrine building with a well-maintained place of worship. === Festival === A list of festivals held annually at Yoshida Shrine . * Monthly festival (1st and 15th of every month) * New Year's Day Festival ({{ill|lt=January 1st|Q2150|WD=Q2150}}) * Setsubun Festival ({{ill|lt=February 3rd|Q2315|WD=Q2315}}) * Open-air tea ceremony (around late March) * Spring Festival ({{ill|lt=April 29th|Q2535|WD=Q2535}}) * Great Purification ({{ill|lt=June 30th|Q2657|WD=Q2657}}) * Rice planting festival, children's andon festival (late July) * Reitaisai (mid-October) *: In the old days, it was held on {{ill|lt=September 15th (lunar calendar)|Q840070|WD=Q840070|lt=September 15th of the lunar calendar}}. A grand mikoshi crossing to the Naka River is held {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. * Shichi-Go-San prayer (during November) * Labor Thanksgiving Day ({{ill|lt=November 23rd|Q3021|WD=Q3021}}) * Great Purification ({{ill|lt=December 31st|Q2912|WD=Q2912}}) === Cultural assets === * Yoshida Shrine Documents *: Hitachi's leading shrine document {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (National History)|1993}}. The original was destroyed in the war, but a manuscript remains in the Shokokan collection {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (National History)|1993}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}{{ill|lt=Mito City|Q204249|WD=Q204249}}3193-2 Miyauchicho '''Access''' * Nearest station: {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}}, {{ill|lt=Kashima Rinkai Railway|Q5370740|WD=Q5370740}} {{ill|lt=Mito Station|Q909499|WD=Q909499}} **Walk: Approximately 25 minutes ** Bus: Get off at “Yoshida Jinja-mae” bus stop (short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Yoshida Tumulus - National historic site. * Yakuoin ({{Coord|36|21|21.74|N|140|28|39.50|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Former Jinguji Temple: Yakuoin}}) - Former {{ill|lt=Jinguji Temple|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book '''Historical materials''' * "Chinjucho" (included in "Shinto Taikei") *: Shrine book under the Mito domain after the organization of shrines conducted by the Mito domain during the Kanbun and Genroku years (1661-1703). The exact year of establishment is unknown. * "Shinhachisha Ryakuenshi" (included in "Shinto Taikei") *: Written by Shuto Ishii. January 1797. A document that examines 28 Shikinai companies in Hitachi Province. * “Hitachi 28 Shako” (included in “Shinto Taikei”) *: Written by Nobutaka Aoyama. December 1799. A document that examines 28 Shikinai companies in Hitachi Province. * “Suifu Gei-on Koroki” *: Written by Taneaki Takakura. {{ill|lt=Hitachikuni|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya}}
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |alias= |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 13th|Q2925|WD=Q2925}} (Autumn regular festival) |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=42|lon_deg=140|lon_min=28|lon_sec=55|label=Yoshida Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|21|42|N|140|28|55|E}}}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Yoshida Jinja Mitoshi Torii.jpg|220px|thumb|
Ichino Torii
]] '''Yoshida Shrine'' (Yoshida Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}{{ill|lt=Mito City|Q204249|WD=Q204249}} Miyauchi Town. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}), {{ill|lt=Hitachikuni|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya}}, {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Overview === It is located on top of Mt. Asahi, a small hill in the southern part of central Mito city. Its founding tells of {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru}}'s involvement in the eastern expedition. Because it enshrines the god of war, Japan Takeru, it is a shrine that has long been highly revered by the Imperial Court during the {{ill|lt=Ezo|Q932574|WD=Q932574}} conquest process. Although the shrine building was burnt down in the war, a place related to Nihon Takeru still remains within the precincts as ``Mt. Sankaku.'' === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) *: The 12th {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723}} prince and father of the 14th {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}}. According to "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031}}" and "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}," he subjugated the powerful clans of Kyushu and eastern regions on the orders of Emperor Keiko. === History === === Founding === According to ``Joyo Shikinai Chinza Honki'' and ``Hitachi Twenty-eight Shrine Considerations,'' it is said that {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru}} rested his soldiers at this place (Mt. Asahi/Mt. Misumi) during his eastern expedition, and the shrine was built in honor of this {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. The date of its establishment is unknown, but according to ancient documents in the possession of Yoshida Shrine, it was enshrined in {{ill|lt=Shoan|Q1327427|WD=Q1327427}}4 ({{ill|lt=1301|Q5610|WD=Q5610}}). There is a note stating that more than 800 years have passed {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}[http://www.yoshidajinja.jp/hist.html]. The regular autumn festival held on October 19 and 20, 1985 was held as a celebration of the 1500th anniversary of the founding of the company.Iharaki Shimbun, morning edition of October 21, 1985. . The location of the shrine's resting place is still told today in a corner of the temple grounds. Concerning this tradition, there is a theory that the connection to Nihon Takeru dates back to the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, since there is no story about Nippon Takeru in Naga District in ``{{ill|lt=Hitachikuni Fudoki|Q11481687|WD=Q11481687}}'' {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. === Outline === First seen in national history is {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa}} In 13 years ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454}}), he was ranked as a junior fifth rank, lower rank, and eighth rank. It is written that ``Yoshida God'' in } was listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha #Meishin Name|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Meishin}} {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. After that, the divine rank was {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} first year ({{ill|lt=857|Q30374|WD=Q30374}}), and was given the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Rank, and Eighth Rank, {{jalink|Jōgan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}}5 years ({{ill|lt=863 years|Q25052|WD=Q25052}}) Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Rank 8th Class, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Genkei|1=UNKNOWN}}2 ({{ill|lt=878|Q23910|WD=Q23910}}), he was promoted to Seishi Rank, Lower Rank, 8th Class {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}.{{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) {{ill|lt=In the Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shin Name Book}}, "Yoshida Shrine It is listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}{{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. In addition, Yoshida Shrine is {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|WD=Q706499}} ({{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}) in Hitachi Province, {{ill|lt=Shizukami It is said to have been ranked as the third shrine after Shri|Q11663648|WD=Q11663648}} (Ninomiya), and was built in the year 2000, following Kashima Shrine {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system|2000|p=233}}. The reason why it was ranked 3rd in Hitachi Province and 1st in Naka District (Naka District) is said to be due to its relationship with Japan Takeru, the enshrined god of war, and the Ezo conquest, as well as its relationship with the decline of the Ujibe clan in Naga District, which was dominant in the west of Mito.{{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}In the Middle Ages, ``Yoshida County'' was separated from Naga County as the {{ill|lt=Kamigun|Q11590937|WD=Q11590937}} of Yoshida Shrine, but the territory of Yoshida Shrine was 158 towns, covering half of Yoshida County {{Sfn|Yoshida County (Taira)|1982}}. From then on, this shrine territory strengthened its character as a manor, and was also called ``Yoshida Manor'' {{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}}. In addition, we call Yakuo-in Temple {{ill|lt=Jingu-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}, and it is said that the shrine had 6-ken and 3-ken corridors, torii gates, and tamagaki, as well as many attached buildings and subsidiary shrines {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}, and that the shrine reached the rank of Shoichii {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. After that, Yakuo-in flourished as the center of {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|WD=Q668188}}, controversy increased, and the divine power declined {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, he was deeply revered by the {{ill|lt=Mito domain|Q1146904|WD=Q1146904}}, especially {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsukuni Tokugawa|1=UNKNOWN}} repaired the main shrine, worship hall, and many other shrine buildings in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}}6 ({{ill|lt=1666|Q7585|WD=Q7585}}).{{Sf n|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}, {{ill|lt=Nariaki Tokugawa|Q1323770|WD=Q1323770}} was founded in {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}}15 ({{jalink {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}} who donated ``{{ill|lt=Dainippon History|Q1156003|WD=Q1156003}}'' and 100 koku shrine territory to |1844|Q7633}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in April of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q90139226}} in {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}. In {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}}20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240}}), the entire shrine building was burnt down in an air raid {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. The shrine building was rebuilt in 1948 ({{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|WD=Q5165}}), and has undergone subsequent renovations and repairs to its current state {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. === God level === Record of {{ill|lt=Divine Rank|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}} dedication in {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}}. Both names are written as ``Yoshida God''. * {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jowa}} On April 17, 13 years ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454}}), he was entrusted to the Meishin as a junior fifth rank, lower rank, and eighth rank. * {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} May 26, 1st year ({{ill|lt=857|Q30374|WD=Q30374}}), from the 8th grade of the Junior Fifth Rank to the 8th grade of the Lower Rank (Junior 4th Rank, Lower Rank, 8th Grade) * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052}}) August 2nd, from the 8th grade of the junior fourth rank to the 8th grade of the superior rank of the junior fourth rank (``{{ill|lt=Journal of the Third Generation of Japan|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}'') * August 8, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=878|Q23910|WD=Q23910}}), from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Rank, Eighth Class to Sei Fourth Rank, Lower Rank, Lower Rank, Eighth Class (``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'') === Shinto priest === Yoshida Shrine's social affairs are said to have been handled by ``Kimikoji'' in ancient times {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}. The origin and personality of the Yoshimi-kou clan are not clear, but it is said that he belonged to the Emishi tribe in the early Nara period, and there is also a theory that points to a deep relationship with the Emishi {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. After that, Mr. Yoshimiko was appointed to the Central Government during the {{ill|lt=Chojo|Q1194264|WD=Q1194264}} years ({{ill|lt=1132|Q19582|WD=Q19582}}-{{ill|lt=1135|Q19594|WD=Q19594}}). {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}{{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}} who donated a social affairs position (Ryoke position) to the family'''{{ill|lt=Otsuki clan|Q11462133|WD=Q11462133}}'''. This donation is said to have been made to protect the shrine's territory from the interference of local government officials and the invasion of local clans {{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}}. Through subsequent changes, there were five former shrine families (the Tadokoro clan, the Akutsu clan, the Sakaki clan, the Watanabe clan, and the Inoue clan) before the Meiji period. After the Shake system was abolished by the Constitution of the Empire of Japan, the Tadokoro clan was in charge until the middle of the Meiji period, and the Akutsu clan thereafter. After the enactment of the Religious Corporations Law after the war, the Akutsu clan was in charge until the early Heisei period after passing through several Shinto priests, and the Tadokoro clan is currently in charge. === Precincts === The main shrine has {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} copper plate roofing, and the worship hall and the shrine have {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025}} copper plate roofing, both of which were rebuilt after the war {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. On the approach to the shrine, there is a place consecrated as the ``Asahi Sankakuyama Ruins'' where the enshrined deity Nihon Takeru rested {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. This triangular mountain is said to be the site of the main shrine and rituals {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. File:Yoshida Jinja Mitoshi Honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Yoshida-jinja (Mito) keidai.JPG|Precincts File:Yoshida Jinja Mitoshi Zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon File:Yoshida-jinja (Mito) goshinseki.JPG|Asahi Sankakuyama Ruins === Setsusha === === Betsugu === [[File:Kasahara-koyasu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|
Kasahara Yasu Shrine (Kasahara Town)
]] [[File:Sakado-jinja (Mito) haiden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|
Sakamon Shrine (Sakamon Town)
]] "Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi" describes Kasahara Daimyojin and Sakamon Daimyojin as separate shrines. ; Kasahara Yasu Shrine (Kasahara Daimyojin) :* Enshrinement location: Ichiban, Kasahara-cho, Mito City ({{Coord|36|21|5.17|N|140|27|18.13|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Bekku: Kasaharayasu Shrine}}) :* Deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ototachibanahime|UNKNOWN|lt=younger brother Tachibanahime}}, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuyahime|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuyahime}}: A separate shrine of Yoshida Shrine and one of the seven shrines in Mito. During the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}} years ({{ill|lt=1661|Q7005|WD=Q7005}}-{{ill|lt=1673|Q7617|WD=Q7617}}), a shrine was built at the current location on the orders of the founder of the Mito domain, {{ill|lt=Yorifusa Tokugawa|Q1133680|WD=Q1133680}}, and it was said to be the guardian deity of safe childbirth. It is said that there was a tradition of borrowing a statue from the head office when giving birth, and then returning it and offering it after a safe delivery. The offspring were Chinami, Mikawa, and Kasahara. Kasahara Shrine became a religious corporation in September 1952 ({{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272}}), and is now known as ``Kasahara Yasu Shrine'' under the jurisdiction of the umbrella organization {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|WD=Q908077}}. : According to the documents owned by Yoshida Shrine, on February 24, {{ill|lt=Enkei (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkei}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1309|Q5726|WD=Q5726}}), the then Saemon-jo and Uemon-jo issued a reply regarding ``Yoshida Kasahara Shrine Transfer Shrine.'' Furthermore, on May 25, 1996 ({{ill|lt=1396|Q6405|WD=Q6405}}), Ujiyoshi Sani sent out a letter of donation stating, ``Kasahara Gosha Gotodensho Hōdonoya'' (from ``Suifu Geion Koroki''). ; Sakamon Shrine (Sakamon Daimyojin) :* Enshrinement location: Sakamon-cho, Mito City ({{Coord|36|21|22.47|N|140|29|52.64|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Bekku: Sakamon Shrine}}) :* Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuyahime|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohana Sakuyahime}} === Precincts Suesha === * Rokusha (on the right side facing the main shrine) ** Matsuo Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302}} in {{ill|lt=Kyoto|Q740246|WD=Q740246}}. ** Taga Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091}} in {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga}}. ** Sumiyoshi Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|WD=Q11381857}} in {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906}}. ** Hachiman Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Hachiman Shrine|Q11390939|WD=Q11390939}}. ** Okunishushi Daishu Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Okunishu no Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Okunishushi no Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Kotoshiro no Mikoto|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshiro no Mikoto}}. ** Small Pox Guardian Shrine * Two shrines (back right facing the main shrine) *: A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. Both companies are mentioned in the ``Chinjucho'' and ``Jinhachisha Ryakuenshi.'' ** Kunimi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Hikokuni Mikaki Kenyotsuka no Mikoto. {{ill|lt=Ise Jingu|Q687168|WD=Q687168}}He is the enshrined deity of Geku Setsusha and {{ill|lt=Watari Kunomi Shrine|Q11486118|WD=Q11486118}}, and the ancestor of Ise Kunizo and Watarai Shinto priests. ** Hayatoshi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Ryodoirihime no Mikoto. One of the wives of Takeru of Japan and the mother of Emperor Chuai. * Two shrines (back left facing the main shrine) *: A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. Both companies are mentioned in the ``Chinjucho'' and ``Jinhachisha Ryakuenshi.'' ** Ii Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971}}. He was the second prince of Japan Takeru, and his mother was Ryodo Irihime no Mikoto.**: "Shingusha" is written in "Chinjucho". The ``Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi'' describes it as ``Ii Shrine'' and ``Kehi Myojin''. ** Mito Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhiko|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayaakitsuhiko no Mikoto}}. A branch of Mito Shrine in Kasahara-cho, Mito City. * Two shrines (Kodaijingu Shrine and Toyouke Daijingu Shrine) *: "Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi" says "Ise Nisho Bekku". A branch of {{ill|lt=Ise Jingu|Q687168|WD=Q687168}}{{ill|lt=Kōtai Jingu|Q11581011|WD=Q11581011|lt=Naiku}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Geku|1=UNKNOWN}}. The shrine building is Nigensha {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}}. ** Kodai Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu}}. ** Toyouke Daijingu Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Toyouke Hime|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Hime no Mikoto}}. * Inari Shrine/Hashi Shrine *: In "Shuhachisha Ryoenshi", it is only described as {{ill|lt=Inari God|Q719665|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Myojin}}. A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. * Hoshimiya Shrine * Tenmangu - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|WD=Q382005}} *: ``Chinjucho'' says ``Tenjin Shrine,'' and ``Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi'' says ``Tenma Tenjin.'' While all the other subsidiary shrines have small shrine buildings, Tenmangu Shrine has a medium-sized, flowing-style shrine building with a well-maintained place of worship. === Festival === A list of festivals held annually at Yoshida Shrine . * Monthly festival (1st and 15th of every month) * New Year's Day Festival ({{ill|lt=January 1st|Q2150|WD=Q2150}}) * Setsubun Festival ({{ill|lt=February 3rd|Q2315|WD=Q2315}}) * Open-air tea ceremony (around late March) * Spring Festival ({{ill|lt=April 29th|Q2535|WD=Q2535}}) * Great Purification ({{ill|lt=June 30th|Q2657|WD=Q2657}}) * Rice planting festival, children's andon festival (late July) * Reitaisai (mid-October) *: In the old days, it was held on {{ill|lt=September 15th (lunar calendar)|Q840070|WD=Q840070|lt=September 15th of the lunar calendar}}. A grand mikoshi crossing to the Naka River is held {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. * Shichi-Go-San prayer (during November) * Labor Thanksgiving Day ({{ill|lt=November 23rd|Q3021|WD=Q3021}}) * Great Purification ({{ill|lt=December 31st|Q2912|WD=Q2912}}) === Cultural assets === * Yoshida Shrine Documents *: Hitachi's leading shrine document {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (National History)|1993}}. The original was destroyed in the war, but a manuscript remains in the Shokokan collection {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (National History)|1993}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273}}{{ill|lt=Mito City|Q204249|WD=Q204249}}3193-2 Miyauchicho '''Access''' * Nearest station: {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}}, {{ill|lt=Kashima Rinkai Railway|Q5370740|WD=Q5370740}} {{ill|lt=Mito Station|Q909499|WD=Q909499}} **Walk: Approximately 25 minutes ** Bus: Get off at “Yoshida Jinja-mae” bus stop (short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Yoshida Tumulus - National historic site. * Yakuoin ({{Coord|36|21|21.74|N|140|28|39.50|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Former Jinguji Temple: Yakuoin}}) - Former {{ill|lt=Jinguji Temple|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book '''Historical materials''' * "Chinjucho" (included in "Shinto Taikei") *: Shrine book under the Mito domain after the organization of shrines conducted by the Mito domain during the Kanbun and Genroku years (1661-1703). The exact year of establishment is unknown. * "Shinhachisha Ryakuenshi" (included in "Shinto Taikei") *: Written by Shuto Ishii. January 1797. A document that examines 28 Shikinai companies in Hitachi Province. * “Hitachi 28 Shako” (included in “Shinto Taikei”) *: Written by Nobutaka Aoyama. December 1799. A document that examines 28 Shikinai companies in Hitachi Province. * “Suifu Gei-on Koroki” *: Written by Taneaki Takakura. '''Books''' * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Shikiuchi Shrine Study Group|author=Harutomo Numabe|authorlink=Harutomo Numabe|year=1976|chapter=Yoshida Shrine|title=Shikiuchi Shrine Investigation Report Volume 11|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakukan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584}}Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1976}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1982|chapter=|title=Japanese Historical Place Name System 8 Ibaraki Prefecture Place Names|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}|isbn=4582490085|ref=}}** {{Wikicite|reference="Yoshida District"|ref={{Harvid|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Yoshida Shrine"|ref={{Harvid|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Kenichi Tanigawa|editor-link=Kenichi Tanigawa|author=Katsumi Kitabatake|year=1984|chapter=Yoshida Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Places- 11 Kanto|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}}|isbn=4560025118|ref={{Harvid|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Shigeo Itoga|year=1993|chapter=Yoshida Shrine|title={{ill|lt=National History Dictionary (Showa period)|Q11420603|WD=Q11420603|lt=National History Dictionary}} Volume 3|publisher={{ill|lt=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|Q4346755|WD=Q4346755}}|isbn=4642005145|ref={{Harvid|Yoshida Shrine (National History)|1993}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Medieval Countries Ichinomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Research on Medieval Countries Ichinomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Ichinomiya System|2000}}}} === External link === {{ill|lt=Hitachikuni|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353|1=Hitachi Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |alias= |Regular festival = {{ill|lt=October 13th|Q2925|WD=Q2925|1=October 13}} (Autumn regular festival) |Shinto ritual =|Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=42|lon_deg=140|lon_min=28|lon_sec=55|label=Yoshida Shrine|position=top|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|36|21|42|N|140|28|55|E}}}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Yoshida Jinja Mitoshi Torii.jpg|220px|thumb|
Ichino Torii
]] '''Yoshida Shrine'' (Yoshida Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mito City|Q204249|WD=Q204249|1=Mito, Ibaraki}} Miyauchi Town. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), {{ill|lt=Hitachikuni|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353|1=Hitachi Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Overview === It is located on top of Mt. Asahi, a small hill in the southern part of central Mito city. Its founding tells of {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}'s involvement in the eastern expedition. Because it enshrines the god of war, Japan Takeru, it is a shrine that has long been highly revered by the Imperial Court during the {{ill|lt=Ezo|Q932574|WD=Q932574|1=Emishi}} conquest process. Although the shrine building was burnt down in the war, a place related to Nihon Takeru still remains within the precincts as ``Mt. Sankaku.'' === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) *: The 12th {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} prince and father of the 14th {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}}. According to "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}" and "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}," he subjugated the powerful clans of Kyushu and eastern regions on the orders of Emperor Keiko. === History === === Founding === According to ``Joyo Shikinai Chinza Honki'' and ``Hitachi Twenty-eight Shrine Considerations,'' it is said that {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} rested his soldiers at this place (Mt. Asahi/Mt. Misumi) during his eastern expedition, and the shrine was built in honor of this {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. The date of its establishment is unknown, but according to ancient documents in the possession of Yoshida Shrine, it was enshrined in {{ill|lt=Shoan|Q1327427|WD=Q1327427|1=Shōan}}4 ({{ill|lt=1301|Q5610|WD=Q5610|1=1301}}). There is a note stating that more than 800 years have passed {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}[http://www.yoshidajinja.jp/hist.html]. The regular autumn festival held on October 19 and 20, 1985 was held as a celebration of the 1500th anniversary of the founding of the company.Iharaki Shimbun, morning edition of October 21, 1985. . The location of the shrine's resting place is still told today in a corner of the temple grounds. Concerning this tradition, there is a theory that the connection to Nihon Takeru dates back to the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, since there is no story about Nippon Takeru in Naga District in ``{{ill|lt=Hitachikuni Fudoki|Q11481687|WD=Q11481687|1=Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki}}'' {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. === Outline === First seen in national history is {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} In 13 years ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454|1=846}}), he was ranked as a junior fifth rank, lower rank, and eighth rank. It is written that ``Yoshida God'' in } was listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha #Meishin Name|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Meishin|1=Myōjin Taisha}} {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. After that, the divine rank was {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} first year ({{ill|lt=857|Q30374|WD=Q30374|1=857}}), and was given the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Rank, and Eighth Rank, {{jalink|Jōgan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}}5 years ({{ill|lt=863 years|Q25052|WD=Q25052|1=863}}) Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Rank 8th Class, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Genkei|1=UNKNOWN}}2 ({{ill|lt=878|Q23910|WD=Q23910|1=878}}), he was promoted to Seishi Rank, Lower Rank, 8th Class {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}.{{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=In the Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shin Name Book|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, "Yoshida Shrine It is listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}{{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. In addition, Yoshida Shrine is {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|WD=Q706499|1=Kashima Jingū}} ({{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}) in Hitachi Province, {{ill|lt=Shizukami It is said to have been ranked as the third shrine after Shri|Q11663648|WD=Q11663648|1=Shizu Shrine}} (Ninomiya), and was built in the year 2000, following Kashima Shrine {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system|2000|p=233}}. The reason why it was ranked 3rd in Hitachi Province and 1st in Naka District (Naka District) is said to be due to its relationship with Japan Takeru, the enshrined god of war, and the Ezo conquest, as well as its relationship with the decline of the Ujibe clan in Naga District, which was dominant in the west of Mito.{{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}In the Middle Ages, ``Yoshida County'' was separated from Naga County as the {{ill|lt=Kamigun|Q11590937|WD=Q11590937|1=Kamigun}} of Yoshida Shrine, but the territory of Yoshida Shrine was 158 towns, covering half of Yoshida County {{Sfn|Yoshida County (Taira)|1982}}. From then on, this shrine territory strengthened its character as a manor, and was also called ``Yoshida Manor'' {{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}}. In addition, we call Yakuo-in Temple {{ill|lt=Jingu-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}, and it is said that the shrine had 6-ken and 3-ken corridors, torii gates, and tamagaki, as well as many attached buildings and subsidiary shrines {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}, and that the shrine reached the rank of Shoichii {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. After that, Yakuo-in flourished as the center of {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|WD=Q668188|1=Tiantai}}, controversy increased, and the divine power declined {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, he was deeply revered by the {{ill|lt=Mito domain|Q1146904|WD=Q1146904|1=Mito Domain}}, especially {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsukuni Tokugawa|1=UNKNOWN}} repaired the main shrine, worship hall, and many other shrine buildings in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}}6 ({{ill|lt=1666|Q7585|WD=Q7585|1=1666}}).{{Sf n|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}, {{ill|lt=Nariaki Tokugawa|Q1323770|WD=Q1323770|1=Tokugawa Nariaki}} was founded in {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}15 ({{jalink {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}} who donated ``{{ill|lt=Dainippon History|Q1156003|WD=Q1156003|1=Dai Nihonshi}}'' and 100 koku shrine territory to |1844|Q7633}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in April of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} in {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}}), the entire shrine building was burnt down in an air raid {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. The shrine building was rebuilt in 1948 ({{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|WD=Q5165|1=1948}}), and has undergone subsequent renovations and repairs to its current state {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. === God level === Record of {{ill|lt=Divine Rank|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication in {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}}. Both names are written as ``Yoshida God''. * {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jowa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} On April 17, 13 years ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454|1=846}}), he was entrusted to the Meishin as a junior fifth rank, lower rank, and eighth rank. * {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} May 26, 1st year ({{ill|lt=857|Q30374|WD=Q30374|1=857}}), from the 8th grade of the Junior Fifth Rank to the 8th grade of the Lower Rank (Junior 4th Rank, Lower Rank, 8th Grade) * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan|1=Jōgan}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052|1=863}}) August 2nd, from the 8th grade of the junior fourth rank to the 8th grade of the superior rank of the junior fourth rank (``{{ill|lt=Journal of the Third Generation of Japan|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') * August 8, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451|1=Gangyō}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=878|Q23910|WD=Q23910|1=878}}), from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Rank, Eighth Class to Sei Fourth Rank, Lower Rank, Lower Rank, Eighth Class (``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'') === Shinto priest === Yoshida Shrine's social affairs are said to have been handled by ``Kimikoji'' in ancient times {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}. The origin and personality of the Yoshimi-kou clan are not clear, but it is said that he belonged to the Emishi tribe in the early Nara period, and there is also a theory that points to a deep relationship with the Emishi {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. After that, Mr. Yoshimiko was appointed to the Central Government during the {{ill|lt=Chojo|Q1194264|WD=Q1194264|1=Chōshō}} years ({{ill|lt=1132|Q19582|WD=Q19582|1=1132}}-{{ill|lt=1135|Q19594|WD=Q19594|1=1135}}). {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}{{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}} who donated a social affairs position (Ryoke position) to the family'''{{ill|lt=Otsuki clan|Q11462133|WD=Q11462133|1=Ozuki clan}}'''. This donation is said to have been made to protect the shrine's territory from the interference of local government officials and the invasion of local clans {{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}}. Through subsequent changes, there were five former shrine families (the Tadokoro clan, the Akutsu clan, the Sakaki clan, the Watanabe clan, and the Inoue clan) before the Meiji period. After the Shake system was abolished by the Constitution of the Empire of Japan, the Tadokoro clan was in charge until the middle of the Meiji period, and the Akutsu clan thereafter. After the enactment of the Religious Corporations Law after the war, the Akutsu clan was in charge until the early Heisei period after passing through several Shinto priests, and the Tadokoro clan is currently in charge. === Precincts === The main shrine has {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} copper plate roofing, and the worship hall and the shrine have {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} copper plate roofing, both of which were rebuilt after the war {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. On the approach to the shrine, there is a place consecrated as the ``Asahi Sankakuyama Ruins'' where the enshrined deity Nihon Takeru rested {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. This triangular mountain is said to be the site of the main shrine and rituals {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. File:Yoshida Jinja Mitoshi Honden.jpg|Main shrine File:Yoshida-jinja (Mito) keidai.JPG|Precincts File:Yoshida Jinja Mitoshi Zuishinmon.jpg|Zuishinmon File:Yoshida-jinja (Mito) goshinseki.JPG|Asahi Sankakuyama Ruins === Setsusha === === Betsugu === [[File:Kasahara-koyasu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|
Kasahara Yasu Shrine (Kasahara Town)
]] [[File:Sakado-jinja (Mito) haiden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|
Sakamon Shrine (Sakamon Town)
]] "Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi" describes Kasahara Daimyojin and Sakamon Daimyojin as separate shrines. ; Kasahara Yasu Shrine (Kasahara Daimyojin) :* Enshrinement location: Ichiban, Kasahara-cho, Mito City ({{Coord|36|21|5.17|N|140|27|18.13|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Bekku: Kasaharayasu Shrine}}) :* Deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ototachibanahime|UNKNOWN|lt=younger brother Tachibanahime}}, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuyahime|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuyahime}}: A separate shrine of Yoshida Shrine and one of the seven shrines in Mito. During the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=1661|Q7005|WD=Q7005|1=1661}}-{{ill|lt=1673|Q7617|WD=Q7617|1=1673}}), a shrine was built at the current location on the orders of the founder of the Mito domain, {{ill|lt=Yorifusa Tokugawa|Q1133680|WD=Q1133680|1=Tokugawa Yorifusa}}, and it was said to be the guardian deity of safe childbirth. It is said that there was a tradition of borrowing a statue from the head office when giving birth, and then returning it and offering it after a safe delivery. The offspring were Chinami, Mikawa, and Kasahara. Kasahara Shrine became a religious corporation in September 1952 ({{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}}), and is now known as ``Kasahara Yasu Shrine'' under the jurisdiction of the umbrella organization {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. : According to the documents owned by Yoshida Shrine, on February 24, {{ill|lt=Enkei (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkei|1=Enkyō (Kamakura period)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1309|Q5726|WD=Q5726|1=1309}}), the then Saemon-jo and Uemon-jo issued a reply regarding ``Yoshida Kasahara Shrine Transfer Shrine.'' Furthermore, on May 25, 1996 ({{ill|lt=1396|Q6405|WD=Q6405|1=1396}}), Ujiyoshi Sani sent out a letter of donation stating, ``Kasahara Gosha Gotodensho Hōdonoya'' (from ``Suifu Geion Koroki''). ; Sakamon Shrine (Sakamon Daimyojin) :* Enshrinement location: Sakamon-cho, Mito City ({{Coord|36|21|22.47|N|140|29|52.64|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Bekku: Sakamon Shrine}}) :* Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuyahime|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohana Sakuyahime}} === Precincts Suesha === * Rokusha (on the right side facing the main shrine) ** Matsuo Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Kyoto|Q740246|WD=Q740246|1=Kyōto}}. ** Taga Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga|1=Shiga Prefecture}}. ** Sumiyoshi Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|WD=Q11381857|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906|1=Ōsaka}}. ** Hachiman Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Hachiman Shrine|Q11390939|WD=Q11390939|1=Hachiman shrine}}. ** Okunishushi Daishu Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Okunishu no Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Okunishushi no Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}, {{ill|lt=Kotoshiro no Mikoto|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshiro no Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}. ** Small Pox Guardian Shrine * Two shrines (back right facing the main shrine) *: A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. Both companies are mentioned in the ``Chinjucho'' and ``Jinhachisha Ryakuenshi.'' ** Kunimi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Hikokuni Mikaki Kenyotsuka no Mikoto. {{ill|lt=Ise Jingu|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}He is the enshrined deity of Geku Setsusha and {{ill|lt=Watari Kunomi Shrine|Q11486118|WD=Q11486118|1=Watarai Kunimi Shrine}}, and the ancestor of Ise Kunizo and Watarai Shinto priests. ** Hayatoshi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Ryodoirihime no Mikoto. One of the wives of Takeru of Japan and the mother of Emperor Chuai. * Two shrines (back left facing the main shrine) *: A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. Both companies are mentioned in the ``Chinjucho'' and ``Jinhachisha Ryakuenshi.'' ** Ii Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}}. He was the second prince of Japan Takeru, and his mother was Ryodo Irihime no Mikoto.**: "Shingusha" is written in "Chinjucho". The ``Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi'' describes it as ``Ii Shrine'' and ``Kehi Myojin''. ** Mito Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhiko|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayaakitsuhiko no Mikoto}}. A branch of Mito Shrine in Kasahara-cho, Mito City. * Two shrines (Kodaijingu Shrine and Toyouke Daijingu Shrine) *: "Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi" says "Ise Nisho Bekku". A branch of {{ill|lt=Ise Jingu|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}{{ill|lt=Kōtai Jingu|Q11581011|WD=Q11581011|lt=Naiku|1=Kōtai Jingū}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Geku|1=UNKNOWN}}. The shrine building is Nigensha {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}}. ** Kodai Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu}}. ** Toyouke Daijingu Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Toyouke Hime|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Hime no Mikoto}}. * Inari Shrine/Hashi Shrine *: In "Shuhachisha Ryoenshi", it is only described as {{ill|lt=Inari God|Q719665|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Myojin|1=Inari Ōkami}}. A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. * Hoshimiya Shrine * Tenmangu - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} *: ``Chinjucho'' says ``Tenjin Shrine,'' and ``Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi'' says ``Tenma Tenjin.'' While all the other subsidiary shrines have small shrine buildings, Tenmangu Shrine has a medium-sized, flowing-style shrine building with a well-maintained place of worship. === Festival === A list of festivals held annually at Yoshida Shrine . * Monthly festival (1st and 15th of every month) * New Year's Day Festival ({{ill|lt=January 1st|Q2150|WD=Q2150|1=January 1}}) * Setsubun Festival ({{ill|lt=February 3rd|Q2315|WD=Q2315|1=February 3}}) * Open-air tea ceremony (around late March) * Spring Festival ({{ill|lt=April 29th|Q2535|WD=Q2535|1=April 29}}) * Great Purification ({{ill|lt=June 30th|Q2657|WD=Q2657|1=June 30}}) * Rice planting festival, children's andon festival (late July) * Reitaisai (mid-October) *: In the old days, it was held on {{ill|lt=September 15th (lunar calendar)|Q840070|WD=Q840070|lt=September 15th of the lunar calendar|1=September 15 (Lunisolar)}}. A grand mikoshi crossing to the Naka River is held {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. * Shichi-Go-San prayer (during November) * Labor Thanksgiving Day ({{ill|lt=November 23rd|Q3021|WD=Q3021|1=November 23}}) * Great Purification ({{ill|lt=December 31st|Q2912|WD=Q2912|1=December 31}}) === Cultural assets === * Yoshida Shrine Documents *: Hitachi's leading shrine document {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (National History)|1993}}. The original was destroyed in the war, but a manuscript remains in the Shokokan collection {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (National History)|1993}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mito City|Q204249|WD=Q204249|1=Mito, Ibaraki}}3193-2 Miyauchicho '''Access''' * Nearest station: {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}}, {{ill|lt=Kashima Rinkai Railway|Q5370740|WD=Q5370740|1=Kashima Rinkai Railway}} {{ill|lt=Mito Station|Q909499|WD=Q909499|1=Mito Station (Ibaraki)}} **Walk: Approximately 25 minutes ** Bus: Get off at “Yoshida Jinja-mae” bus stop (short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Yoshida Tumulus - National historic site. * Yakuoin ({{Coord|36|21|21.74|N|140|28|39.50|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Former Jinguji Temple: Yakuoin}}) - Former {{ill|lt=Jinguji Temple|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book '''Historical materials''' * "Chinjucho" (included in "Shinto Taikei") *: Shrine book under the Mito domain after the organization of shrines conducted by the Mito domain during the Kanbun and Genroku years (1661-1703). The exact year of establishment is unknown. * "Shinhachisha Ryakuenshi" (included in "Shinto Taikei") *: Written by Shuto Ishii. January 1797. Ichino Torii
]] '''Yoshida Shrine'' (Yoshida Shrine) is located in {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mito City|Q204249|WD=Q204249|1=Mito, Ibaraki}} Miyauchi Town. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), {{ill|lt=Hitachikuni|Q1055353|WD=Q1055353|1=Hitachi Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Sannomiya|1=Ichinomiya}}, {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Overview === It is located on top of Mt. Asahi, a small hill in the southern part of central Mito city. Its founding tells of {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}'s involvement in the eastern expedition. Because it enshrines the god of war, Japan Takeru, it is a shrine that has long been highly revered by the Imperial Court during the {{ill|lt=Ezo|Q932574|WD=Q932574|1=Emishi}} conquest process. Although the shrine building was burnt down in the war, a place related to Nihon Takeru still remains within the precincts as ``Mt. Sankaku.'' === Enshrined God === The enshrined deity is one of the followingShrine Historysho. . * '''{{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}}''' (Yamato Takeru Mikoto) *: The 12th {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|WD=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}} prince and father of the 14th {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}}. According to "{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|WD=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}" and "{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935|1=Nihon Shoki}}," he subjugated the powerful clans of Kyushu and eastern regions on the orders of Emperor Keiko. === History === === Founding === According to ``Joyo Shikinai Chinza Honki'' and ``Hitachi Twenty-eight Shrine Considerations,'' it is said that {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Nippon Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} rested his soldiers at this place (Mt. Asahi/Mt. Misumi) during his eastern expedition, and the shrine was built in honor of this {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. The date of its establishment is unknown, but according to ancient documents in the possession of Yoshida Shrine, it was enshrined in {{ill|lt=Shoan|Q1327427|WD=Q1327427|1=Shōan}}4 ({{ill|lt=1301|Q5610|WD=Q5610|1=1301}}). There is a note stating that more than 800 years have passed {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}[http://www.yoshidajinja.jp/hist.html]. The regular autumn festival held on October 19 and 20, 1985 was held as a celebration of the 1500th anniversary of the founding of the company.Iharaki Shimbun, morning edition of October 21, 1985. . The location of the shrine's resting place is still told today in a corner of the temple grounds. Concerning this tradition, there is a theory that the connection to Nihon Takeru dates back to the early {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, since there is no story about Nippon Takeru in Naga District in ``{{ill|lt=Hitachikuni Fudoki|Q11481687|WD=Q11481687|1=Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki}}'' {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. === Outline === First seen in national history is {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} In 13 years ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454|1=846}}), he was ranked as a junior fifth rank, lower rank, and eighth rank. It is written that ``Yoshida God'' in } was listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha #Meishin Name|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Meishin|1=Myōjin Taisha}} {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. After that, the divine rank was {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} first year ({{ill|lt=857|Q30374|WD=Q30374|1=857}}), and was given the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Rank, and Eighth Rank, {{jalink|Jōgan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jōgan}}5 years ({{ill|lt=863 years|Q25052|WD=Q25052|1=863}}) Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Rank 8th Class, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Genkei|1=UNKNOWN}}2 ({{ill|lt=878|Q23910|WD=Q23910|1=878}}), he was promoted to Seishi Rank, Lower Rank, 8th Class {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}.{{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended|1=Enchō}} "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927 years|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}) {{ill|lt=In the Engishiki Shrine Name Book|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shin Name Book|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, "Yoshida Shrine It is listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}{{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. In addition, Yoshida Shrine is {{ill|lt=Kashima Shrine|Q706499|WD=Q706499|1=Kashima Jingū}} ({{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}) in Hitachi Province, {{ill|lt=Shizukami It is said to have been ranked as the third shrine after Shri|Q11663648|WD=Q11663648|1=Shizu Shrine}} (Ninomiya), and was built in the year 2000, following Kashima Shrine {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system|2000|p=233}}. The reason why it was ranked 3rd in Hitachi Province and 1st in Naka District (Naka District) is said to be due to its relationship with Japan Takeru, the enshrined god of war, and the Ezo conquest, as well as its relationship with the decline of the Ujibe clan in Naga District, which was dominant in the west of Mito.{{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}In the Middle Ages, ``Yoshida County'' was separated from Naga County as the {{ill|lt=Kamigun|Q11590937|WD=Q11590937|1=Kamigun}} of Yoshida Shrine, but the territory of Yoshida Shrine was 158 towns, covering half of Yoshida County {{Sfn|Yoshida County (Taira)|1982}}. From then on, this shrine territory strengthened its character as a manor, and was also called ``Yoshida Manor'' {{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}}. In addition, we call Yakuo-in Temple {{ill|lt=Jingu-ji|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}, and it is said that the shrine had 6-ken and 3-ken corridors, torii gates, and tamagaki, as well as many attached buildings and subsidiary shrines {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}, and that the shrine reached the rank of Shoichii {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikinai Shrine)|1976}}. After that, Yakuo-in flourished as the center of {{ill|lt=Tendai sect|Q668188|WD=Q668188|1=Tiantai}}, controversy increased, and the divine power declined {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, he was deeply revered by the {{ill|lt=Mito domain|Q1146904|WD=Q1146904|1=Mito Domain}}, especially {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitsukuni Tokugawa|1=UNKNOWN}} repaired the main shrine, worship hall, and many other shrine buildings in {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}}6 ({{ill|lt=1666|Q7585|WD=Q7585|1=1666}}).{{Sf n|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}, {{ill|lt=Nariaki Tokugawa|Q1323770|WD=Q1323770|1=Tokugawa Nariaki}} was founded in {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}15 ({{jalink {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}} who donated ``{{ill|lt=Dainippon History|Q1156003|WD=Q1156003|1=Dai Nihonshi}}'' and 100 koku shrine territory to |1844|Q7633}}.After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}}, in April of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}), it was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} in {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. In {{ill|lt=Showa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}}20 ({{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|WD=Q5240|1=1945}}), the entire shrine building was burnt down in an air raid {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. The shrine building was rebuilt in 1948 ({{ill|lt=1948|Q5165|WD=Q5165|1=1948}}), and has undergone subsequent renovations and repairs to its current state {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. === God level === Record of {{ill|lt=Divine Rank|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} dedication in {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}}. Both names are written as ``Yoshida God''. * {{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jowa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} On April 17, 13 years ({{ill|lt=846|Q30454|WD=Q30454|1=846}}), he was entrusted to the Meishin as a junior fifth rank, lower rank, and eighth rank. * {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} May 26, 1st year ({{ill|lt=857|Q30374|WD=Q30374|1=857}}), from the 8th grade of the Junior Fifth Rank to the 8th grade of the Lower Rank (Junior 4th Rank, Lower Rank, 8th Grade) * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan|1=Jōgan}} 5th year ({{ill|lt=863|Q25052|WD=Q25052|1=863}}) August 2nd, from the 8th grade of the junior fourth rank to the 8th grade of the superior rank of the junior fourth rank (``{{ill|lt=Journal of the Third Generation of Japan|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'') * August 8, {{ill|lt=Genkei|Q747451|WD=Q747451|1=Gangyō}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=878|Q23910|WD=Q23910|1=878}}), from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Rank, Eighth Class to Sei Fourth Rank, Lower Rank, Lower Rank, Eighth Class (``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'') === Shinto priest === Yoshida Shrine's social affairs are said to have been handled by ``Kimikoji'' in ancient times {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}. The origin and personality of the Yoshimi-kou clan are not clear, but it is said that he belonged to the Emishi tribe in the early Nara period, and there is also a theory that points to a deep relationship with the Emishi {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. After that, Mr. Yoshimiko was appointed to the Central Government during the {{ill|lt=Chojo|Q1194264|WD=Q1194264|1=Chōshō}} years ({{ill|lt=1132|Q19582|WD=Q19582|1=1132}}-{{ill|lt=1135|Q19594|WD=Q19594|1=1135}}). {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Taira)|1982}}{{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}} who donated a social affairs position (Ryoke position) to the family'''{{ill|lt=Otsuki clan|Q11462133|WD=Q11462133|1=Ozuki clan}}'''. This donation is said to have been made to protect the shrine's territory from the interference of local government officials and the invasion of local clans {{Sfn|Yoshida District (Taira)|1982}}. Through subsequent changes, there were five former shrine families (the Tadokoro clan, the Akutsu clan, the Sakaki clan, the Watanabe clan, and the Inoue clan) before the Meiji period. After the Shake system was abolished by the Constitution of the Empire of Japan, the Tadokoro clan was in charge until the middle of the Meiji period, and the Akutsu clan thereafter. After the enactment of the Religious Corporations Law after the war, the Akutsu clan was in charge until the early Heisei period after passing through several Shinto priests, and the Tadokoro clan is currently in charge. === Precincts === The main shrine has {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} copper plate roofing, and the worship hall and the shrine have {{ill|lt=irimoya-zukuri|Q855025|WD=Q855025|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} copper plate roofing, both of which were rebuilt after the war {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. On the approach to the shrine, there is a place consecrated as the ``Asahi Sankakuyama Ruins'' where the enshrined deity Nihon Takeru rested {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1976}}. This triangular mountain is said to be the site of the main shrine and rituals {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. Sakamon Shrine (Sakamon Town)
]] "Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi" describes Kasahara Daimyojin and Sakamon Daimyojin as separate shrines. ; Kasahara Yasu Shrine (Kasahara Daimyojin) :* Enshrinement location: Ichiban, Kasahara-cho, Mito City ({{Coord|36|21|5.17|N|140|27|18.13|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Bekku: Kasaharayasu Shrine}}) :* Deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ototachibanahime|UNKNOWN|lt=younger brother Tachibanahime}}, {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuyahime|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuyahime}}: A separate shrine of Yoshida Shrine and one of the seven shrines in Mito. During the {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} years ({{ill|lt=1661|Q7005|WD=Q7005|1=1661}}-{{ill|lt=1673|Q7617|WD=Q7617|1=1673}}), a shrine was built at the current location on the orders of the founder of the Mito domain, {{ill|lt=Yorifusa Tokugawa|Q1133680|WD=Q1133680|1=Tokugawa Yorifusa}}, and it was said to be the guardian deity of safe childbirth. It is said that there was a tradition of borrowing a statue from the head office when giving birth, and then returning it and offering it after a safe delivery. The offspring were Chinami, Mikawa, and Kasahara. Kasahara Shrine became a religious corporation in September 1952 ({{ill|lt=1952|Q5272|WD=Q5272|1=1952}}), and is now known as ``Kasahara Yasu Shrine'' under the jurisdiction of the umbrella organization {{ill|lt=Shinja Honcho|Q908077|WD=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. : According to the documents owned by Yoshida Shrine, on February 24, {{ill|lt=Enkei (Japan)|Q1327068|WD=Q1327068|lt=Enkei|1=Enkyō (Kamakura period)}} 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1309|Q5726|WD=Q5726|1=1309}}), the then Saemon-jo and Uemon-jo issued a reply regarding ``Yoshida Kasahara Shrine Transfer Shrine.'' Furthermore, on May 25, 1996 ({{ill|lt=1396|Q6405|WD=Q6405|1=1396}}), Ujiyoshi Sani sent out a letter of donation stating, ``Kasahara Gosha Gotodensho Hōdonoya'' (from ``Suifu Geion Koroki''). ; Sakamon Shrine (Sakamon Daimyojin) :* Enshrinement location: Sakamon-cho, Mito City ({{Coord|36|21|22.47|N|140|29|52.64|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Bekku: Sakamon Shrine}}) :* Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuyahime|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohana Sakuyahime}} === Precincts Suesha === * Rokusha (on the right side facing the main shrine) ** Matsuo Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302|1=Matsunoo Taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Kyoto|Q740246|WD=Q740246|1=Kyōto}}. ** Taga Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Taga Taisha|Q402091|WD=Q402091|1=Taga-taisha}} in {{ill|lt=Shiga Prefecture|Q131358|WD=Q131358|lt=Shiga|1=Shiga Prefecture}}. ** Sumiyoshi Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|Q11381857|WD=Q11381857|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Osaka|Q11439906|WD=Q11439906|1=Ōsaka}}. ** Hachiman Shrine - A branch of {{ill|lt=Hachiman Shrine|Q11390939|WD=Q11390939|1=Hachiman shrine}}. ** Okunishushi Daishu Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Okunishu no Mikoto|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Okunishushi no Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi}}, {{ill|lt=Kotoshiro no Mikoto|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshiro no Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}. ** Small Pox Guardian Shrine * Two shrines (back right facing the main shrine) *: A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. Both companies are mentioned in the ``Chinjucho'' and ``Jinhachisha Ryakuenshi.'' ** Kunimi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Hikokuni Mikaki Kenyotsuka no Mikoto. {{ill|lt=Ise Jingu|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}He is the enshrined deity of Geku Setsusha and {{ill|lt=Watari Kunomi Shrine|Q11486118|WD=Q11486118|1=Watarai Kunimi Shrine}}, and the ancestor of Ise Kunizo and Watarai Shinto priests. ** Hayatoshi Shrine - Enshrined deity: Ryodoirihime no Mikoto. One of the wives of Takeru of Japan and the mother of Emperor Chuai. * Two shrines (back left facing the main shrine) *: A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. Both companies are mentioned in the ``Chinjucho'' and ``Jinhachisha Ryakuenshi.'' ** Ii Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Emperor Chuai|Q179971|WD=Q179971|1=Emperor Chūai}}. He was the second prince of Japan Takeru, and his mother was Ryodo Irihime no Mikoto.**: "Shingusha" is written in "Chinjucho". The ``Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi'' describes it as ``Ii Shrine'' and ``Kehi Myojin''. ** Mito Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Hayaakitsuhiko|UNKNOWN|lt=Hayaakitsuhiko no Mikoto}}. A branch of Mito Shrine in Kasahara-cho, Mito City. * Two shrines (Kodaijingu Shrine and Toyouke Daijingu Shrine) *: "Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi" says "Ise Nisho Bekku". A branch of {{ill|lt=Ise Jingu|Q687168|WD=Q687168|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}{{ill|lt=Kōtai Jingu|Q11581011|WD=Q11581011|lt=Naiku|1=Kōtai Jingū}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Geku|1=UNKNOWN}}. The shrine building is Nigensha {{ill|lt=Shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}}. ** Kodai Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu}}. ** Toyouke Daijingu Shrine - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Toyouke Hime|UNKNOWN|lt=Toyouke Hime no Mikoto}}. * Inari Shrine/Hashi Shrine *: In "Shuhachisha Ryoenshi", it is only described as {{ill|lt=Inari God|Q719665|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari Myojin|1=Inari Ōkami}}. A simple shrine building built with a terraced structure. * Hoshimiya Shrine * Tenmangu - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Michizane Sugawara|Q382005|WD=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} *: ``Chinjucho'' says ``Tenjin Shrine,'' and ``Shuhachisha Ryakuenshi'' says ``Tenma Tenjin.'' While all the other subsidiary shrines have small shrine buildings, Tenmangu Shrine has a medium-sized, flowing-style shrine building with a well-maintained place of worship. === Festival === A list of festivals held annually at Yoshida Shrine . * Monthly festival (1st and 15th of every month) * New Year's Day Festival ({{ill|lt=January 1st|Q2150|WD=Q2150|1=January 1}}) * Setsubun Festival ({{ill|lt=February 3rd|Q2315|WD=Q2315|1=February 3}}) * Open-air tea ceremony (around late March) * Spring Festival ({{ill|lt=April 29th|Q2535|WD=Q2535|1=April 29}}) * Great Purification ({{ill|lt=June 30th|Q2657|WD=Q2657|1=June 30}}) * Rice planting festival, children's andon festival (late July) * Reitaisai (mid-October) *: In the old days, it was held on {{ill|lt=September 15th (lunar calendar)|Q840070|WD=Q840070|lt=September 15th of the lunar calendar|1=September 15 (Lunisolar)}}. A grand mikoshi crossing to the Naka River is held {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (Gods)|1984}}. * Shichi-Go-San prayer (during November) * Labor Thanksgiving Day ({{ill|lt=November 23rd|Q3021|WD=Q3021|1=November 23}}) * Great Purification ({{ill|lt=December 31st|Q2912|WD=Q2912|1=December 31}}) === Cultural assets === * Yoshida Shrine Documents *: Hitachi's leading shrine document {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (National History)|1993}}. The original was destroyed in the war, but a manuscript remains in the Shokokan collection {{Sfn|Yoshida Shrine (National History)|1993}}. === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Mito City|Q204249|WD=Q204249|1=Mito, Ibaraki}}3193-2 Miyauchicho '''Access''' * Nearest station: {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East|1=East Japan Railway Company}}, {{ill|lt=Kashima Rinkai Railway|Q5370740|WD=Q5370740|1=Kashima Rinkai Railway}} {{ill|lt=Mito Station|Q909499|WD=Q909499|1=Mito Station (Ibaraki)}} **Walk: Approximately 25 minutes ** Bus: Get off at “Yoshida Jinja-mae” bus stop (short walk after getting off) '''Surroundings''' * Yoshida Tumulus - National historic site. * Yakuoin ({{Coord|36|21|21.74|N|140|28|39.50|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Former Jinguji Temple: Yakuoin}}) - Former {{ill|lt=Jinguji Temple|Q1074971|WD=Q1074971|1=Jingū-ji}}. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} {{reflist}} === References === * Shrine history book '''Historical materials''' * "Chinjucho" (included in "Shinto Taikei") *: Shrine book under the Mito domain after the organization of shrines conducted by the Mito domain during the Kanbun and Genroku years (1661-1703). The exact year of establishment is unknown. * "Shinhachisha Ryakuenshi" (included in "Shinto Taikei") *: Written by Shuto Ishii. January 1797. A document that examines 28 Shikinai companies in Hitachi Province. * “Hitachi 28 Shako” (included in “Shinto Taikei”) *: Written by Nobutaka Aoyama. December 1799. A document that examines 28 Shikinai companies in Hitachi Province. * “Suifu Gei-on Koroki” *: Written by Taneaki Takakura.
